title: Everyday Dharma
definition: The recognition that profound spiritual practice and insight occur within ordinary, even mundane, experiences — not only in retreat or formal meditation. A can of Coca-Cola can serve as a complete teaching on craving, impermanence, and liberation. The marketplace is the monastery; the carbonated beverage is the sutra.

metadata.creator: Hieros-CADMIES
metadata.genesis: Identified by human co-gardener from conversation about Coca-Cola consumption and Buddhist practice, May 2026

difficulty_levels.beginner: You don't need a meditation cushion or a quiet room. The can of Coke in your hand right now is already a perfect teacher — just pay attention to what's happening.
difficulty_levels.intermediate: Everyday Dharma dissolves the sacred/profane binary. The same principles discovered in formal practice — impermanence, craving, liberation — are fully present in a sip of soda. Practice is not a place you go; it's a way of relating to whatever is already happening.
difficulty_levels.expert: This principle aligns with the Mahayana teaching that samsara and nirvana are not separate realms. The Coke is empty of inherent existence, dependently arisen, and a complete dharma gate. Awakening is not elsewhere — it is available in the most ordinary moments of consumption and sensation.

extra_fields.historical_context: Everyday Dharma emerged within contemporary Buddhism as a response to the need for integrating spiritual practice into modern life.
limitations: The principle may be interpreted in ways that trivialize or oversimplify spiritual practice.
It could potentially blur the lines between spiritual and mundane activities, leading to a lack of focus on formal practice.

axioms: Any moment of experience contains the entirety of the Dharma.
Retreat from the world is not required for awakening.
Ordinary objects and experiences can be complete vehicles for insight.
The distinction between 'sacred practice' and 'mundane life' is itself an attachment.
poetic_version: The clink of ice, the fizz on the tongue — it's all part of the Dharma.
